content:
# Joshua Chapter 20 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), page 244 | **Source Image:** `../../source-texts/hebrew-OT/JOS_020_page_001.jpg`

---

**1** YHWH said to Joshua, **2** "Tell the Sons of Israel: Designate the cities of refuge that I told you about through Moses — **3** places where someone who kills another person accidentally, without intent, can flee for safety. These cities will protect you from the blood avenger.

**4** "When someone flees to one of these cities, he must stand at the entrance of the city gate and present his case to the elders of that city. They must take him in, give him a place to live, and let him settle among them. **5** If the blood avenger comes in pursuit, they must not hand the killer over, because he struck his neighbor unintentionally — there was no prior hatred between them. **6** He must remain in that city until he has stood trial before the community, and until the high priest serving at that time has died. Then the killer may go back to his own city and his own home — the city he originally fled from."

**7** So they consecrated Kedesh in the Galilee, in the hill country of Naphtali; Shechem in the hill country of Ephraim; and Kiriath-arba (that is, Hebron) in the hill country of Judah. **8** And east of the Jordan, across from Jericho, they designated Bezer in the wilderness plateau from the tribe of Reuben; Ramoth in Gilead from the tribe of Gad; and Golan in Bashan from the tribe of Manasseh.

**9** These were the designated cities for all the Sons of Israel and for any foreigner living among them, so that anyone who killed a person accidentally could flee there and not die at the hands of the blood avenger before standing trial before the community.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**v.2 — "cities of refuge" (arei ha-miqlat):** The Hebrew miqlat comes from a root meaning "to receive, to take in." These are sanctuary cities — safe harbors established by divine command. The institution was first outlined in Numbers 35 and Deuteronomy 19; here Joshua implements it.

**v.3 — "accidentally, without intent" (bishgagah bivli da'at):** The Hebrew uses two overlapping phrases: shegagah ("error, inadvertence") and bivli da'at ("without knowledge"). PNEUMA merges them into natural English rather than preserving the redundancy, since both phrases make the same legal point — this was not murder.

**v.3 — "blood avenger" (go'el ha-dam):** The go'el is normally the "kinsman-redeemer" — the nearest male relative obligated to act on behalf of family. In the context of a killing, his role shifts to avenger. The institution of refuge cities exists precisely to restrain this cycle of retaliatory violence and replace it with judicial process.

**v.5 — "no prior hatred" (lo soneh hu lo mittemol shilshom):** Literally "he was not hating him from yesterday and the day before" — a standard Hebrew idiom meaning "there was no history of enmity." PNEUMA renders the meaning rather than the idiom.

**v.6 — "until the high priest has died":** One of the most striking legal provisions in the Torah. The unintentional killer's exile is bound not to a fixed sentence but to the lifespan of the serving high priest. His death functions as a kind of release — an amnesty that ends the exile. The theological resonance (priestly death bringing freedom) is deep but unstated in the text itself; PNEUMA does not import it.

**v.7 — "they consecrated" (vayaqdishu):** The Hebrew root q-d-sh means "to make holy, to set apart." These cities were not merely assigned — they were sanctified for their protective purpose. PNEUMA uses "consecrated" to preserve this sacral dimension.

**v.7 — "Kiriath-arba (that is, Hebron)":** The text includes its own explanatory gloss. Kiriath-arba was the older Canaanite name; Hebron was the name familiar to later readers. PNEUMA renders this parenthetical naturally.

**v.8 — The six cities:** Three west of the Jordan (Kedesh, Shechem, Hebron) and three east (Bezer, Ramoth, Golan) — one in each major tribal region, ensuring that a refuge city was within reasonable reach from anywhere in the land. The geographic balance is deliberate and thorough.

**v.9 — "any foreigner living among them" (la-ger ha-gar betokham):** The Hebrew ger is the resident alien — a non-Israelite living permanently within the community. The refuge law applies to them equally. This is a remarkable statement of legal inclusion: justice does not depend on ethnicity.

— PNEUMA
